segments.inferno-34-g21: Quando introduces a temporal clause (“when”). Fummo fatti is passato remoto of essere (fummo) + past participle fatti; here this periphrasis expresses that “we were made/caused” to be in a certain position — idiomatically “we had come/advanced” — and avante is an adverb meaning “forward/so far on.”
segments.inferno-34-g22: ch’ is the contraction of che introducing the content of the temporal clause (“that”). Al mio maestro piacque is an impersonal or experiencer construction with piacque (passato remoto of piacere) meaning “it pleased my master”; di mostrarmi is an infinitive complement (di + mostrar‑ + enclitic mi = “to show me”). The relative clause la creatura ch’ebbe il bel sembiante uses ebbe (passato remoto of avere) to mean “that had” and sembiante = “appearance/countenance.”
segments.inferno-34-g23: D’innanzi = di innanzi “from/in front of.” Mi si tolse is a reflexive/passive-type construction with tolse (passato remoto of togliere) and si; literally “it removed itself from before me” or “it was taken away from before me,” with mi marking the affected person. E fé restarmi: fé (past form of fare) + infinitive restarmi (restare + enclitic mi) = “and made me remain/stand still.”
segments.inferno-34-g24: Ecco is an interjection (“behold/here is”). Dite is the proper name Dis (the ruler of the infernal city); the phrase is a direct quotation naming the place/figure revealed.
segments.inferno-34-g25: Dicendo is the present participle of dire and here connects the speech to the preceding action: “saying.” It indicates that what follows was uttered as the action occurred.
segments.inferno-34-g26: Ed ecco il loco = “and behold the place.” Ove introduces a relative/adverbial clause “where.” Convien is an impersonal present (“it is necessary/fit”) and che introduces a subjunctive clause; t’armi is t’ (enclitic you) + armi, present subjunctive 2nd singular of armare meaning “that you arm/prepare yourself.” Di fortezza indicates the means or quality (“with fortitude/strength”) — literally “arm yourself of/with fortitude.”
segments.inferno-34-g27: Com’ is the contracted poetic form of Come meaning “how.” Io is the pronoun “I.” Divenni is the passato remoto (past definite) of diventare, “I became.” Allor (then) is an adverb placed after the verb for rhythm. Gelato e fioco are predicate adjectives describing his state (‘frozen’/numb and ‘faint’/dim) following the verb.
segments.inferno-34-g28: Nol is the clitic combination of non + lo (literally “not it”) used here with an imperative sense: ‘do not ask about it.’ Dimandar is the infinitive of chiedere/ dimandare (to ask) used in commands or prohibitions in older/poetic Italian instead of a modern negative imperative.
segments.inferno-34-g29: Lettor is the vocative ‘reader.’ The final -e is dropped for metrical reasons (poetic truncation), so this is a direct address.
segments.inferno-34-g30: Ch’ is the clitic of che here introducing a causal/explanatory clause (“for/because”). I’ (io) is the subject pronoun ‘I.’ Non is the sentential negation. Lo is the direct object clitic ‘it,’ referring to the matter just mentioned. Scrivo is the present indicative ‘I write’ (Dante uses the present to report his refusal to describe).
segments.inferno-34-g31: Però here introduces the reason (roughly ‘for’/‘because’); ch’ again is a conjunction ‘that/for.’ Ogne (archaic of ogni) means ‘every’ or ‘all.’ Parlar is the infinitive used substantively: ‘speech’ or ‘words.’ Sarebbe is the conditional of essere, ‘would be.’ Poco means ‘little’ or ‘insufficient’ — the whole phrase says that any description would be inadequate.
segments.inferno-34-g32: Io is the subject pronoun ‘I.’ Non mori’ is the past tense (passato remoto) morì ‘I died’; the apostrophe marks the dropped accent in the manuscript spelling. The second clause uses e non rimasi vivo: rimasi is passato remoto of rimanere ‘I remained/was left,’ and vivo is the adjective ‘alive.’ The phrasing expresses that he was neither dead nor living.
segments.inferno-34-g33: Pensa is an imperative (or hortative) meaning ‘think’ or ‘consider.’ Oggimai is an adverb meaning ‘nowadays/by now’ or here simply ‘now.’ Per te means ‘for you’ or ‘for your sake,’ with per marking benefit/purpose.
segments.inferno-34-g34: S’ is the archaic written form of se (‘if’). Hai is the present second-person singular of avere, ‘you have.’ Fior is metaphorical ‘flower,’ i.e. the best part or spark (here of wit). D’ is the contraction of di ‘of.’ Ingegno means ‘intellect/ingenuity’ — the phrase asks the reader, if he has any wit, to imagine what happened.
segments.inferno-34-g35: Qual is a relative/interrogative ‘what’ introducing the content of the reader’s imagination: ‘what I became.’ Io again is the subject pronoun. Divenni is passato remoto ‘I became.’ This is a resumptive clause pointing back to the prior description.
segments.inferno-34-g36: D’uno and d’altro are contractions of di uno e di altro, literally ‘of one and of the other.’ Privo is an adjective meaning ‘deprived’ or ‘bereft’; here it predicates the speaker (implicitly ‘I’) and agrees in sense with that subject: ‘bereft of both one and the other.’ The construction lists two things he lacked after stating he was neither dead nor alive.
segments.inferno-34-g37: Lo 'mperador = the emperor (article + noun; 'mperador' is Dante's spelling of 'imperador'/'imperatore'). 'del doloroso regno' is a genitive phrase 'of the dolorous/sorrowful realm'. 'da mezzo 'l petto' literally 'from the middle of the chest' (da + mezzo + the elided article 'l' before petto) indicates the extent up to mid‑chest. 'uscia' is an archaic/dialectal past form of uscire (here 'came out'); 'fuor de la ghiaccia' = 'out of the ice' (fuor = out, de = of/from, la ghiaccia = the ice).
segments.inferno-34-g38: 'e più' = 'and moreover' (più adds 'further' or 'besides'). 'con un gigante' = 'with a giant'. 'io mi convegno' = 'I come together / I meet' — 'mi' is the reflexive pronoun and 'convegno' is first‑person singular of convegnare (here used in the sense 'I meet / I encounter' or 'I am joined with'). The clause is in the narrative (Dante often uses a present or historical present here).
segments.inferno-34-g39: 'che' introduces an explanatory/relative clause referring back to that giant: 'for/so that'. 'i giganti non fan' = 'the giants do not make/do' (non before the verb negates it). 'con le sue braccia' = 'with his arms' and here functions as a comparative/instrumental phrase: the clause is elliptical (the object is left implicit) and means roughly 'with whose arms even the giants do not compare / which no giants can match.'
segments.inferno-34-g40: 'vedi oggimai' = 'see now / observe by now' (vedi here is an imperative or hortatory present addressing the listener/reader; oggimai = by now). 'quant' = how great / how much. 'esser dee' = 'must be' — 'dee' is an archaic form of dovere (modal 'must/ought') governing the infinitive 'esser' (to be). 'quel tutto' = 'that whole' and the relative clause 'ch’a così fatta parte si confaccia' = 'that must correspond/befit such a made part': 'a' = 'to', 'così fatta' = 'so made/so large (lit. thus made)', 'parte' = 'part', 'si confaccia' = reflexive use of confacciare ('to befit/fit') (here meaning 'properly corresponds to').
segments.inferno-34-g127: Di là = 'from/on that side' (di + locative là). Fosti is the second-person singular past (passato remoto) of essere 'you were' (here poetic/older form used without auxiliary). Cotanto ... quant' is a correlative comparative: cotanto (so far/to such an extent) ... quant' io scesi (as much as I descended); quant' is an elided form of quanto used in comparisons.
segments.inferno-34-g128: Quand' = quando (elided before a vowel) meaning 'when'. Io mi volsi is a simple past (passato remoto) 'I turned' with the reflexive pronoun mi; volsi is first-person singular. The comma shows this is a temporal subordinate clause setting the time for what follows.
segments.inferno-34-g129: Tu passasti is passato remoto 'you passed' (second-person singular). '’l punto' = 'the point' (elided il). 'Al qual' = 'at/on which' (a + il + quale); si traggon is an impersonal/passive construction (si + third-person plural traggon) meaning 'are drawn' or 'are dragged' — si here makes the verb general/impersonal. 'D’ogne parte i pesi' means 'the weights from every side': d'ogne = 'from every', parte = 'side/part', i pesi = 'the weights' (subject of traggon in the impersonal sense).
segments.inferno-34-g130: E se’ or = E (and) + se’ (archaic contraction of sei, second-person singular present “you are”) with or (now) omitted in the word list; the clause places the subject (you) before the predicate. sotto l’emisperio giunto is a participial description: giunto (past participle ‘arrived/reached’) after the noun emisperio (‘hemisphere’) describes the state of the subject (you) — literally ‘you are arrived beneath the hemisphere.’ ch’ è contraposto a quel che la gran secca coverchia is a relative clause introduced by ch’ (che ‘that/which’) with è (is) + contraposto (past participle/adjectival ‘opposed/placed opposite’) followed by a to-phrase a quel che… (‘to that which…’).
segments.inferno-34-g131: e sotto ’l cui colmo consunto links to the previous clause: e (and) + sotto (under) + ’l (the) + cui (relative pronoun ‘whose/of which’) + colmo consunto (‘summit/peak, worn away’ — colmo is the noun ‘summit’ and consunto is a past participle used adjectivally). fu l’uom che nacque e visse sanza pecca is a simple past statement: fu (was) + l’uom (the man) followed by a relative clause che nacque e visse (who was born and lived — both verbs in the passato remoto) and sanza pecca (a prepositional phrase ‘without sin’).
segments.inferno-34-g132: tu haï i piedi in su picciola spera is a present-clause statement: tu (you) + haï (archaic spelling of hai, second-person singular present ‘have’) + i piedi (your feet) + in su (upon/on) + picciola spera (a small sphere — note spera is a poetic spelling for sfera ‘sphere’). che l’altra faccia fa de la Giudecca is a relative clause: che (which/that) + l’altra faccia (the other face/side) + fa (makes/forms) + de la Giudecca (of/from the Giudecca).
segments.inferno-34-g133: Qui è da man is a short locative clause: Qui (here) + è (is) + da man. The idiom da man (= da mano) means ‘on the (right) hand/side’ (literally ‘from/by the hand’); da here is a locative/prepositional use and man is ‘hand’ used idiomatically for side.
segments.inferno-34-g134: quando di là è sera is a temporal subordinate: quando (when) + di là (over there/on the other side — di is the preposition used with là) + è (is) + sera (evening/dusk). The whole phrase means ‘when it is evening on the far side.’
segments.inferno-34-g135: e questi, is simply the connective e (and) followed by questi (these/those here) as the subject/topic of the following relative clause.
segments.inferno-34-g136: che ne fé scala col pelo is a relative clause modifying questi: che (who/which) + ne (of it/from it) referring back to some preceding thing + fé (fecé, archaic/fixed poetic past ‘made’) + scala (here a noun ‘ladder’ formed from the verb-noun scal(a); the provided lemma list gives verbal senses, but the word functions as the noun ‘ladder’) + col (con il, ‘with the’) + pelo (hair). Grammatically, it says they ‘made a ladder of it with their hair.’
segments.inferno-34-g137: fitto è ancora sì come prim’ era is a comment on their current state: fitto (firmly fixed/compact/dense) + è (is) + ancora (still) + sì come (just as/as) + prim’ (prima, formerly/previously; elided vowel) + era (was). The construction compares their present compactness to how they were before.
segments.inferno-34-g138: Da + noun phrase (questa parte) = 'from this side'. 'Da' is the preposition 'from'; 'questa' is the feminine singular demonstrative modifying 'parte'. 'Cadde' is the passato remoto (simple past) of cadere, 'he fell', and 'giù' is a postverbal adverb 'down'. 'Dal cielo' is the contraction da + il = 'from the heaven/sky'.
segments.inferno-34-g139: Simple coordinate phrase: 'e la terra' = 'and the earth'. 'E' = 'and', 'la' is the definite article before the feminine noun 'terra'.
segments.inferno-34-g140: 'che' is the relative pronoun referring back to 'la terra' ('which'). 'Pria' is an archaic adverb meaning 'before/previously' (the provided lemma list did not include this sense, so it is marked as not matched). 'Di qua' = 'on this side' (di + qua), and 'si sporse' is the passato remoto reflexive form of sporgersi ('to project/jut out'), with 'si' reflexive and 'sporse' the past tense.
segments.inferno-34-g141: 'Per paura di lui' gives the cause: 'because of fear of him' — 'per' indicating cause, 'paura' = 'fear', 'di lui' = 'of him'. 'Fé' is the literary passato remoto of fare ('made/caused'), used here causatively: the earth 'made' a 'velo' (veil/covering) out of the sea: 'del mar velo' = 'a veil of the sea' (del = di + il, 'mar' is the poetic form of 'mare').
segments.inferno-34-g142: 'E venne a l’emisperio nostro' = 'and it came to our hemisphere'. 'Venne' is past of venire ('came/arrived'); 'a' = 'to'; 'l’ emisperio' = 'the hemisphere' (elided article), and 'nostro' is the possessive 'our'. The subject remains 'la terra'.
segments.inferno-34-g143:
This coordinate clause begins with 'e forse' ('and perhaps'). 'Per fuggir lui' uses the infinitive 'fuggir' with 'per' of purpose = 'to flee him'; 'lui' is the object 'him'. 'Lasciò' is past of lasciare ('left'), 'qui' = 'here', 'loco vòto' = 'an empty place' ('vòto' = empty). 'Quella ch'appar di qua' is a relative clause: 'quella' = 'that (thing)', 'ch
pp ar' = contracted 'che appare' ('which appears'), 'di qua' = 'on this side'. The whole phrase says the earth perhaps left an empty spot here that appears on this side.
segments.inferno-34-g144: 'E sù ricorse' = 'and ran/returned back up'. 'E' = 'and', 'sù' is an adverb 'up', and 'ricorse' is the passato remoto of ricorrere used here to mean 'returned' or 'ran back' upward; the clause closes the reported speech (note the closing quotation mark in the original).
segments.inferno-34-g1: 'Vexilla regis' is a Latin-style noun phrase meaning 'the banners of the king' (vexilla is plural); 'prodeunt' is a 3rd-person plural present indicative 'they come forth/advance'; 'inferni' here supplies the domain ('of Hell') so the whole phrase means 'the banners of the King of Hell.' 'verso di noi' is a directional phrase 'toward us' with verso 'toward' and di noi marking the object of the direction.
segments.inferno-34-g2: Però is an adverb meaning 'therefore/so'; dinanzi is an adverb 'ahead/in front'; mira is an imperative (2nd person singular) of mirare, 'look' or 'behold' addressed to the reader/listener. The phrase orders the addressee to look forward.
segments.inferno-34-g3: Disse is passato remoto (3rd singular) of dire: 'said.' '’l' is the elided form of il 'the,' and 'maestro mio' is the subject phrase 'my master/teacher' (Virgil); together 'disse ’l maestro mio' = 'my master said.'
segments.inferno-34-g4: Se introduces a conditional clause 'if'; tu is the explicit 2nd-person subject 'you'; discerni is 2nd-person singular present (indicative) of discernere 'discern, make out' — here 'if you can/may discern it.' Note the object pronoun 'l' (it) is omitted in the segment but referenced earlier.
segments.inferno-34-g5: Come quando = 'as when'; una grossa nebbia is a simple noun phrase 'a thick/large fog'; spira is 3rd-person singular present of spirare 'blows' or 'breathes' (here meteorological: fog blowing or swelling). The line sets up a simile.
segments.inferno-34-g6: O quando = 'or when'; l’ emisperio nostro = 'our hemisphere' (l' = the); annotta is a poetic/historical verb meaning 'grows dark' or 'is nighted' (Dante's nonstandard/poetic formation). The clause offers an alternative condition in the simile.
segments.inferno-34-g7: Par is 3rd-person singular present of parere 'it seems'; di lungi is an adverbial phrase 'from afar'; un molin 'a mill' is the grammatical subject of par; che introduces a relative clause 'that the wind turns' (che + subject + verb). '’l vento gira' = 'the wind turns it.'
segments.inferno-34-g8: 'Veder mi parve' is an infinitive used as the object/subject with parve: literally 'to see seemed to me' — a common Italian construction meaning 'I seemed to see.' 'mi' is the indirect object 'to me'; 'parve' is passato remoto 'seemed'; 'un tal edificio' = 'such a building'; allotta = 'then/thereupon' (poetic adverb).
segments.inferno-34-g9: Poi = 'then/afterwards'; per lo vento = 'because of/by the wind' (per here causal/agentive); mi ristrinsi retro = 'I drew myself back' (ristrinsi = 1st singular past, reflexive sense 'contracted/withdrew'); al duca mio = 'behind/to my leader' (a + il contracted = al). The whole clause says the speaker recoiled behind his guide due to the wind.
segments.inferno-34-g10: Ché is a conjunction meaning 'for/because'; non lì era = 'there was not' (non + lì + era 'was'); altra grotta = 'another grotto/cave.' The clause explains the reason for withdrawing.
segments.inferno-34-g11: Già is an adverb 'already'; era is the imperfect/past 'was' (the narrative past — 'it already was/existed'). The subject is omitted because it is clear from the context.
segments.inferno-34-g12: E con paura = 'and with fear'; il metto in metro = 'I put it into metre/verse' (metto = 1st person present 'I put' used here in a narrative sense; il = 'it' direct object referring back to the scene; in metro = 'into meter/poetic measure').
segments.inferno-34-g13: Là dove = 'there where'; l'ombre tutte = 'the shades/all the shadows' (subject); eran coperte = imperfect 'were covered' (eran = were, coperte = past participle used adjectivally). The clause gives the location/condition of the shades.
segments.inferno-34-g14: E trasparien = 'and they showed through/ were transparent' (3rd-person plural imperfect referring back to 'ombre'); come festuca in vetro = a simile 'like a straw in glass' (festuca = a small straw or chaff, vetro = glass) — the image stresses faint, glassy visibility.
segments.inferno-34-g15: Altre (feminine plural) = 'others'; sono a giacere uses essere + a + infinitive (sono a giacere) to indicate state 'are lying down' or 'are in a position of lying.' The construction describes one group of the shades.
segments.inferno-34-g16: Altre = 'others'; stanno (3rd pl present of stare) = 'stand' or 'remain'; erte (feminine plural of erto) = 'upright/erect' — describing a different posture of the shades.
segments.inferno-34-g17: Quella ... e quella ... = 'that one with the head and that one with the soles' — quella refers back to one of the previously mentioned groups; col is the contraction of con + il 'with the'; capo = 'head'; con le piante = 'with the soles' (piante = the soles of the feet here). The line contrasts different orientations.
segments.inferno-34-g18: Altra = 'another' (feminine singular), introducing the last example of posture.
segments.inferno-34-g19: Com' (elided form of come) = 'like/as'; arco = 'a bow' or 'arch' — a simile comparing the body's curve to a bow.
segments.inferno-34-g20: Il volto = 'the face'; a’ piè is a contracted form of a + i (to the feet) meaning 'toward the feet'; rinverte is 3rd-person singular present of rinvertire/rinverta 'turn back/ bend over' — 'turns the face toward the feet.' The verb agrees with the singular 'altra' introduced earlier.
segments.inferno-34-g41: S’el is a contracted, archaic form corresponding to modern se 'l (if he/it). Fu is the passato remoto of essere (he was). Sì ... com’ sets up a comparison ('so ... as'), with bel (beautiful) before the noun/adjective as a normal poetic adjective. Elli is an archaic pronoun referring to that person/being ('he'/'it') and è ora brutto states a present-time contrast ('is now ugly'); ora is the temporal 'now'. The whole clause is a conditional protasis (If he was so beautiful ...).
segments.inferno-34-g42: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. Contra (poetic variant of contro) means 'against' and governs the following noun phrase '’l suo fattore' (il suo fattore = his Maker/Creator), where ’l is the elided definite article. Alzò is the passato remoto 'he raised'; le ciglia is the direct object ('the eyelashes/eyelids' i.e. raised his brows) and is a literal body-part object.
segments.inferno-34-g43: Ben here is an adverbial intensifier ('indeed'/'surely'). Dee is an archaic present form of dovere ('must/ought to'). It takes the infinitive procedere ('to proceed/originate'), so the construction ben dee procedere = 'ought surely to proceed/originate'. Da lui marks source ('from him'), and ogne lutto is the object ('every grief/mourning').
segments.inferno-34-g44: Oh quanto parve a me gran maraviglia is an exclamation: parve (passato remoto, 'it seemed') has the experiencer marked by a me (a me = 'to me'), and gran maraviglia is the thing that appeared ('a great wonder'). Quand' io vidi is a temporal clause ('when I saw') with the elided quando. Tre facce a la sua testa places three faces as located on his head; a + la here is a locative use meaning 'on' or 'upon'.
segments.inferno-34-g45: L’una is the contracted form of la + una used as a pronoun meaning “the one” or “one of them”; dinanzi is an adverb placed after the noun and means “in front” or “facing forward.” Together the phrase states which of the faces is situated to the front.
segments.inferno-34-g46: e links this clause to the previous one (“and”); quella is a demonstrative pronoun (“that one”) referring back to L’una; era is the imperfect indicative of essere (“was”), a past descriptive tense; vermiglia is an adjective (feminine) agreeing with quella meaning “vermilion/ruddy.”
segments.inferno-34-g47: l’altr’ is an elided form of gli altri / le altre used here as “the others”; eran is the archaic imperfect plural (modern erano) of essere, so “they were.” The segment states that the remaining two faces form the other group.
segments.inferno-34-g48: che introduces a relative clause referring to the two others (“which”/“who”); s’aggiugnieno is an archaic third-person plural past/imperf. reflexive/reciprocal form (si aggiunsero / they joined themselves) meaning “they joined” or “were joined to”; a questa sovresso l’mezzo di ciascuna spalla literally places that joining “to this one above the middle of each shoulder,” where sovresso = sopra di (on/over) and mezzo di ciascuna spalla = the midpoint of each shoulder.
segments.inferno-34-g49: e links to the next action (“and”); sé is the reflexive pronoun (themselves) emphasizing reciprocal joining; giugnieno is the same archaic 3rd pl. form as above (they met/reached); al loco de la cresta = “at the place of the crest,” with al (a + il) marking motion toward that location and de la cresta = of the crest/top of the head.
segments.inferno-34-g50: e la destra parea = “and the right (side/hand) seemed,” where parea is the imperfect of parere/parere (appeared/seemed). tra bianca e gialla places the color between white and yellow (tra = between); bianca and gialla are coordinating adjectives describing the hue.
segments.inferno-34-g51: la sinistra a vedere era tal = “the left, to see (i.e. in appearance), was such,” where a vedere is an idiomatic phrase meaning “to the eye” or “in appearance,” era is imperfect “was,” and tal (tale) = “such” introduces the forthcoming simile.
segments.inferno-34-g52: quali begins the comparison (“such as”); the omitted vegnon (vengono) that follows in the text means “come”/“that come”; di là = “from there/that region”; onde here functions as “where”/“whence” (introducing the place clause) and should be read as “from where”; ’l Nilo is the Nile; s’avvalla is a present/indicative reflexive sense of avvallare meaning “slopes down” or “descends.” The whole phrase compares the left face’s color to people (or things) coming from where the Nile slopes down.
segments.inferno-34-g53: Sotto ciascuna = “under each (one),” where ciascuna refers back to each head/side; uscivan is an archaic imperfect plural (uscivano) meaning “came out” or “projected”; due grand’ ali = “two great wings,” with grand’ an elided form of grandi so it agrees in number with ali.
segments.inferno-34-g54: quanto si convenia a tanto uccello = a fixed idiom meaning “as much as was fitting for such a bird”; quanto is “as much,” si convenia is an impersonal/reflective imperfect of convenire (“was appropriate”/“suited”), and a tanto uccello specifies the referent “for so great a bird.”
segments.inferno-34-g55: vele di mar non vid’ io mai cotali is an inversion common in Dante: vid’ io (vidi io) = “I saw” with vid’ an elided past tense, preceded by the negator non and followed by mai (here conveying “ever” in the negative sense = “never”); vele di mar = “sails of the sea” (i.e., seafaring sails) and cotali = “such as these.” The line states the poet has never seen sails like those at sea.
segments.inferno-34-g68: Quell’ is the shortened form of quella (the demonstrative 'that') elided before a vowel; anima is the noun 'soul'; là sù are two locative adverbs stacked (là = 'there', sù = 'above'), giving 'up there'. The sequence c’ ha is a contracted colloquial/poetic form of che ha ('who/that has'), linking the relative clause to anima; maggior pena is a noun phrase 'greater/most suffering' (maggior = comparative 'greater', pena = 'suffering/punishment').
segments.inferno-34-g69: disse is the passato remoto (simple past narrative) of dire, 'he said' — typical for storytelling; ’l is the elided form of the definite article il (here written with an apostrophe as in medieval/poetic orthography); maestro is the noun 'master' (Virgil, the guide). Together they form the speaker tag "said the master."
segments.inferno-34-g70: è is the present indicative 3rd‑person singular of essere, the copula 'is'; Giuda Scarïotto is the proper name 'Judas Iscariot' (Scarïotto shows an archaic/poetic spelling with diaeresis to mark pronunciation). This segment is a simple predication: 'is Judas Iscariot.'
segments.inferno-34-g71: che is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a relative clause modifying Giuda; 'l is the definite article il (elided); capo is 'head' and is the direct object of ha ('has'), so che 'l capo ha dentro = 'who has his head inside'. e fuor introduces a contrast ('and outside'), and le gambe is the noun phrase 'the legs' which is syntactically linked to mena: mena (from menare) is 3rd‑person singular present 'he leads/carries/moves'. In older/poetic Italian the two actions are coordinated (he has the head inside and carries the legs outside).
segments.inferno-34-g72: De li altri due is an archaic/poetic order for 'of the other two' (de = di 'of/from'); c’ hanno is a contracted form of che hanno ('who have') introducing the relative clause; il capo di sotto literally 'the head beneath' = 'their heads below' (di sotto = 'under, below'). The phrase describes which of the two is meant.
segments.inferno-34-g73: quel che introduces 'the one that...' (quel = 'that one'); pende is present indicative 'hangs' (from pendere); dal is the contraction of da + il = 'from the'; nero ceffo = 'black/dark face' (ceffo = ugly face/visage); è Bruto is the copular clause 'is Brutus.' The colon that follows marks the coming demonstration or exhortation.
segments.inferno-34-g74: vedi is the 2nd‑person singular imperative or present indicative 'see/look' used here as an address to Dante ('see how...'); come = 'how/as'; si is the reflexive pronoun used with storce to form an intransitive reflexive 'writhe/twist oneself'; storce is present indicative 'twists/writhes'. The clause invites the listener to observe Brutus's contortions.
segments.inferno-34-g75: e = 'and'; non is the negation 'not'; fa is the 3rd‑person singular present of fare 'does/makes'; motto here means 'a sound/word/utterance' (i.e., any cry). The phrase non fa motto = 'he does not make a sound' (he makes no cry).
segments.inferno-34-g76: This segment is punctuation only (a semicolon continuing the narrator's speech) and contains no lexical items to analyze.
segments.inferno-34-g77: e = 'and'; l’altro is the elided form of 'the other' (l' = il + altro); è = 'is'; Cassio = the proper name 'Cassius'. This names the second of the two figures just described.
segments.inferno-34-g78: che is the relative pronoun 'who/that'; par is an archaic/poetic form of pare (3rd‑person singular present) meaning 'seems/appears'; sì = 'so' (intensifier here: 'so/beside that'); membruto is an adjective meaning 'large‑limbed' or 'brawny'. Together: 'who appears so big‑limbed.'
segments.inferno-34-g79: Ma = 'but'; la notte = 'the night'; risurge is present indicative (or narrative present) of risorgere 'rises again/returns' — a temporal remark signaling the approach of morning. The clause sets the temporal reason for leaving.
segments.inferno-34-g80: e = 'and'; oramai = 'by now/now at last' (temporal adverb); è da partir is an impersonal/modal expression meaning 'it is time to leave' or literally 'it is to depart' (è + da + infinitive indicates necessity or suitability): è (copula) + da (marker) + partir (infinitive 'to leave').
segments.inferno-34-g81: ché = 'for/because' (introducing the reason); tutto = 'all/everything'; avem is the archaic 1st‑person plural present perfect auxiliary (avem = abbiamo) used with a past participle; veduto is the past participle 'seen'. Together: 'for we have seen everything.'
segments.inferno-34-g56: Non is the negation particle placed before the verb. Avean is an archaic imperfect 3rd-person plural of avere (modern avevano), here meaning 'they had'; penne is a plural noun (direct object) meaning 'feathers'. Word order is standard negation + verb + object.
segments.inferno-34-g57: Ma = 'but' (coordinating conjunction). Di + vispistrello forms a genitive/qualifying phrase 'of a bat' used adjectivally ('bat-like'); era is imperfect 3rd-person singular of essere because the grammatical subject is modo (singular); lor is the poetic form of loro = 'their' placed before the noun; modo = 'manner' (predicate nominative). The construction literally reads 'but of bat was their manner.'
segments.inferno-34-g58: E = 'and' linking this clause. Quelle is a demonstrative pronoun referring back to the wings ('those ones'); svolazzava is the imperfect 3rd-person singular of svolazzare 'to flutter/beat about'. (Dante allows a collective or metrical agreement here: quelle refers to the wings but the verb appears singular.)
segments.inferno-34-g59: Sì che = 'so that' (sì used to introduce a result). Tre venti = 'three winds' (venti = winds). Si movean is imperfect 3rd-person plural of muovere with the clitic si marking the action (here reflexive/active: 'moved themselves' / 'were set moving'); da ello = 'from it' (da = from; ello = it) indicates the winds originated from that movement.
segments.inferno-34-g60: Quindi = 'therefore' / 'as a result' linking cause and effect. Cocito is the proper name (Cocytus). Tutto here modifies the verb and means 'completely' or 'entirely.' S’ (si) is the enclitic reflexive pronoun attached to the verb aggelava (imperfect 3rd-person singular of aggelare) yielding 'became frozen' or 'grew frozen.'
segments.inferno-34-g61: Con = 'with' introducing an instrument/possession ('with six eyes'). Sei occhi is a noun phrase (sei = six, occhi = eyes). Piangëa is an archaic/poetic imperfect 3rd-person singular of piangere (modern piangeva); the diaeresis marks metric pronunciation but grammatically it is an imperfect verb meaning 'was weeping' or 'wept.'
segments.inferno-34-g62: E = 'and' joining clauses. Per + tre + menti = 'through/throughout three menti' (per here = 'through' or 'by means of'); menti (literally 'minds' in the lemma list, but in context functions as one of the three heads/mouths) is the noun governing the next verb. Gocciava is imperfect 3rd-person singular of gocciare 'to drip/trickle' and takes ’l pianto e sanguinosa bava as its subject(s)/objects depending on reading: 'the tears and bloody drool were dripping.' L (’l) is the article 'the'; pianto = 'tears/weeping' (here 'tears'); sanguinosa = adjective 'bloody'; bava = 'drool/froth' (the lemma senses given don't match this meaning, but here it functions as bodily fluid).
segments.inferno-34-g63: Da = 'from' marking origin. Ogne is the archaic form of ogni = 'every.' Bocca = 'mouth' (here 'from every mouth'). Dirompea is an imperfect/poetic form of dirompere (3rd-person singular) meaning 'burst forth' or 'sprang out'; the verb appears before its subject un peccatore, a stylistic inversion. Co' is the contracted form of con + the following article/word (con + i/e etc.); here co’ denti = 'with (its) teeth' indicating instrument. Un peccatore = 'a sinner' (subject that bursts forth).
segments.inferno-34-g64: A guisa di = a fixed comparative phrase meaning 'in the manner of' or 'like.' Guisa = 'manner'; di = 'of'; maciulla is a tool (a crusher/hemp-brake); here it is a simile comparing the action to a crushing implement.
segments.inferno-34-g65: Sì che = 'so that' introducing result. Tre = 'three' — the phrase tre ne facea literally 'he made three of them': ne is a partitive or clitic referring back to 'them' (of them); facea is imperfect 3rd-person singular of fare used here causatively 'made' or 'caused'; così = 'so/thus'; dolenti = plural adjective 'sorrowful/pained' agreeing with the implied 'three' (tre). Word order is result clause with clitic ne standing for 'of them.'
segments.inferno-34-g66: A quel dinanzi is a deictic/comparative locution: a + quel + dinanzi = 'as to that in front' or 'compared to the one before.' Il mordere and l' graffiar are infinitives used as verbal nouns ('the biting' and 'the scratching'); era nulla = imperfect 'was nothing' (i.e., insignificant); verso = 'compared to/toward' here marking the thing being compared ('the biting was nothing compared to the scratching'). Note orthodox article use: il mordere (the act of biting) vs l' graffiar (the act of scratching).
segments.inferno-34-g67: Che = 'which' introducing a relative clause referring to the scratching. Talvolta = 'sometimes.' La schiena = 'the back' (body-part). Rimanea is an imperfect 3rd-person singular of rimanea (rimaneva) 'remained/was left.' De is an archaic form of di ('of'); de la pelle = 'of the skin' (genitive), and tutta brulla = 'entirely bare/stripped' (tutta modifying brulla to mean 'completely bare'). The clause literally says 'which sometimes the back remained of the skin completely bare' — idiomatically 'which sometimes left the back entirely stripped of skin.'
segments.inferno-34-g102: Io is the subject pronoun 'I'. levai is the passato remoto (simple past) of levare, here 'I raised' or 'I lifted' (an immediate past action). li occhi uses the archaic plural article li + noun (occhi = 'eyes'); credetti is passato remoto of credere, 'I believed' or 'I thought', followed by the infinitive vedere used as its object 'to see' (i.e. 'I believed I saw'). Lucifero is the direct object of vedere; com’ is a shortened form of come 'as/like', and io l’avea lasciato is a subordinate clause: l’ is the clitic (here standing for 'him'), avea is imperfect of avere used with the past participle lasciato to form the pluperfect 'I had left (him)'.
segments.inferno-34-g103: e = 'and' linking the clauses. vidili is passato remoto of vedere, 'I saw'; le gambe is the direct object 'the legs' (le = feminine plural article). in sù is a prepositional phrase 'up' or 'upwards'; tenere is the infinitive 'to hold' used here to describe the state in which the legs were seen (poetic/elliptical infinitive functioning like a participle: 'I saw the legs held up').
segments.inferno-34-g104: e = 'and'. s’ here is a contracted form used for se 'if' (archaic/poetic s’io = se io = 'if I'), not the reflexive si; io = 'I' subject. divenni is passato remoto of divenire 'I became'; allora = 'then'. travagliato is a past participle/adjective meaning 'tormented' or 'distressed' describing the speaker's state after the vision.
segments.inferno-34-g105: la gente grossa is the noun phrase 'the coarse/large crowd' (la = article, gente = 'people', grossa = adjective describing their roughness or size). il here is an enclitic pronoun meaning 'it' (object pronoun referring back to the speaker's state or reaction) and pensi is present subjunctive or jussive third singular of pensare used hortatively/addressing an imagined reader/listener: 'let the crowd think it' or 'let the coarse crowd imagine it'.
segments.inferno-34-g106: che = 'who/that' introducing a relative clause describing that crowd. non vede = 'do not see' (present indicative); qual è quel punto = 'which is that point' or 'what sort of point that was' (qual = which/what kind, è = is, quel punto = that point/moment). ch’ (che) = 'that' introducing the subordinate clause ch’io avea passato: io = 'I', avea + passato = pluperfect 'I had passed' (avea = imperfect auxiliary, passato = past participle of passare).
segments.inferno-34-g107: Lèvati is the imperative reflexive form of levarsi ('get up'/'rise up'); the reflexive ending -ti addresses the speaker directly (second person singular). sù = 'up' or 'upwards', reinforcing the command.
segments.inferno-34-g108: disse is passato remoto 'he said'; ’l is the contracted article clitic (il) used before maestro; maestro = 'master' or 'teacher', here Virgil speaking as Dante’s guide.
segments.inferno-34-g109: in piede is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'on (your) feet' or 'standing'. Grammatically in = preposition and piede = 'foot', but together they instruct Dante to stand up.
segments.inferno-34-g110: la via è lunga = 'the way/road is long' (la = article, via = road/way, è = present of essere 'is', lunga = adjective 'long'). e ’l cammino è malvagio = 'and the path is evil/wicked' (’l = contracted lo, cammino = path/journey, è = is, malvagio = 'malignant/evil' describing the difficulty or danger of the road).
segments.inferno-34-g111: e già = 'and already' (già = 'by now'/'already'). il sole = 'the sun'; a mezza terza is a temporal expression meaning 'at half of the third hour' (a = 'at', mezza = 'half', terza = 'third [hour]' in medieval time reckoning), indicating roughly the time of day. riede is present indicative of riedere (archaic) 'returns' or 'comes back' (here: the sun is already returning).
segments.inferno-34-g112: Non + era is the imperfect copula meaning 'was not'. Camminata is a noun formed from the verb camminare, here meaning a 'passage' or 'walkway' rather than the action of walking. Di palagio is a genitive description 'of (a) palace'; là ’v’ eravam = 'there where we were' (’v’ is a poetic contraction of ove/ov' = 'where') and eravam is the first-person-plural imperfect 'we were' indicating location.
segments.inferno-34-g113: Ma = 'but' introduces the contrast. Natural is an adjective placed before the noun for emphasis — 'natural' as opposed to man-made. Burella is an archaic noun for a small, dark hollow or narrow passage. Ch'avea is a relative + imperfect (avea = aveva) 'that had', linking to its two qualities: mal suolo 'bad ground' (mal modifies suolo: poor, treacherous, or uneven footing) and di lume disagio literally 'of light inconvenience' — a poetically inverted phrase meaning it suffered from a lack of light.
segments.inferno-34-g114: Prima + ch' (contraction of che) introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning "before". De l'abisso = "from the abyss" (de is an archaic/poetic variant of di + the article l'); mi is the clitic object "me"; divella is a 3rd-person present (literary) verb meaning "(he) tears/wrenches away" (Dante often uses the present for immediate action). The whole segment therefore reads literally, "Before he tears me from the abyss."
segments.inferno-34-g115: A short vocative: maestro mio = "my master/teacher." It addresses the speaker's guide (Virgil) directly; the closing quote and comma mark the end of that vocative within the reported speech.
segments.inferno-34-g116: Diss' io is inverted poetical word order (verb before subject); diss' is the past (remote) form "I said" with an apostrophe showing an omitted vowel. Quando fui dritto is a temporal clause: fui is passato remoto of essere "I was / I became" and dritto here means "upright" or "standing up," so: "I said when I stood upright."
segments.inferno-34-g117: This is an imperative request. A introduces purpose: "to" / "in order to"; trarmi is the infinitive trarre + enclitic mi = "to draw/pull me"; d'erro (di erro) = "from error" (Dante uses erro for "error"); un poco "a little" modifies the extent of the relief; mi favella is an imperative "speak to me" (favella used as a (poetic) 2nd-person command). Word order is split for poetic effect: the purpose (a trarmi d'erro un poco) is fronted and favella is the actual command.
segments.inferno-34-g118: Ov' is a contracted form of ove/ove meaning "where"; è is the present of essere "is"; la ghiaccia is an archaic/poetic noun meaning "the ice" or frozen surface. The segment is a direct question: "Where is the ice?"
segments.inferno-34-g119: This is a question introduced by the coordinating conjunction e (and). 'Questi' is a demonstrative pronoun ('these/these ones') referring back to previously mentioned persons or things. 'Com’' is a contracted form of come (elision before the vowel), meaning 'how'; the clause uses the neutral present verb è (3rd singular of essere). 'Fitto' here functions as a past-participle/adjective meaning 'fixed/fastened/tightly set' modifying the implied subject (these), and 'sì sottosopra' is an adverbial phrase 'so upside-down' (sì = so, sottosopra = upside-down) that qualifies how they are fixed.
segments.inferno-34-g120: The simple connective 'e come,' = 'and how,' introduces a follow-up question; come here means 'how'.
segments.inferno-34-g121: 'In sì poc’ ora' is an adverbial time phrase: 'in' introduces the time span, 'sì' = 'so', 'poc’' (poco with elision) = 'a short (while)', and 'ora' = 'time/hour' — together 'in so short a time'. Word order is emphatic/poetic but straightforward.
segments.inferno-34-g122: Temporal phrase 'da sera a mane' = 'from evening to morning' (da…a marks the interval). 'Ha fatto' is present perfect (3rd person singular of avere + past participle fatto) meaning 'has made' or 'has done'; 'il sol tragitto' = 'the sun's journey/route' (il sol is a poetical form for il sole). The whole is a direct question about whether the sun has completed that passage within the short time.
segments.inferno-34-g123: A single punctuation mark: the period/full stop ending the previous question or marking a brief pause in the verse.
segments.inferno-34-g124: 'Ed' is a variant of e (and) used before a vowel for euphony. 'Elli' is an archaic/poetic subject pronoun meaning 'he' (here 'he replied'), and 'a me' marks the indirect object 'to me' (i.e., he said to me). The colon introduces direct speech.
segments.inferno-34-g125: 'Tu imagini ancora d’esser' = 'You still imagine/to suppose yourself to be': 'Tu' subject pronoun, 'imagini' present indicative 'you imagine/suppose', 'ancora' = 'still', and 'd’esser' is the contracted di + esser (di + essere) introducing the infinitive 'to be'. 'Di là dal centro' is a locative expression: di là = 'on the other side/over there' and dal centro = 'from the center' (dal = da + il).
segments.inferno-34-g126: 'Ov’' is an elided form of ove/ove (where). 'Io mi presi' is passato remoto (I seized/took for myself), with the reflexive clitic 'mi' (myself). 'Al pel' = 'by the hair' (al = a + il; pel = pelo/hair). 'Del vermo reo' = 'of the guilty worm' (vermo = worm; reo = guilty/offending), with the adjective after the noun as is common in poetry. The final relative clause introduced by 'che' modifies 'vermo'; 'fóra' is an archaic/poetic form related to forare/foro (to bore/pierce), here meaning 'that had bored/pierced' (i.e., had bored through the world).
segments.inferno-34-g145: Luogo è là giù da Belzebù remoto: a terse, poetic clause with inverted order (Luogo + copula + locative). Luogo (subject) + è (present indicative copula) + the locative phrase là giù da Belzebù (there below, away from Beelzebub). Remoto is an adjectival predicate (past participle/archaic adjective) meaning 'remote/distant' and modifies luogo; tanto quanto la tomba si distende begins the comparative phrase 'as far as...'.
segments.inferno-34-g146: che non per vista,: che is a relative/connecting pronoun referring forward to the place just mentioned; non per vista is an ablative/means phrase ('not by sight'), using per + noun to indicate the means by which it is not known. The clause is elliptical and sets up a contrast with the following 'ma per suono'.
segments.inferno-34-g147: ma per suono è noto d’un ruscelletto che quivi discende per la buca d’un sasso,: ma introduces the contrast ('but'); per suono indicates the means ('by sound') and è noto is present indicative passive-like predication 'is known'. D’un is the contracted di + un; ruscelletto is the source of the sound and the following relative clause (che quivi discende ...) specifies it. Quivi 'there' and discende (present indicative) describe the stream's motion; per la buca 'through the hollow' specifies the path.
segments.inferno-34-g148: ch’elli ha roso,: ch’ (che) introduces a relative clause modifying 'sasso' or the hollow; elli is an archaic/neuter subject pronoun (here referring to the stream as 'it' — medieval usage) and ha roso is a perfect tense (passato prossimo form: 'has gnawed' or 'has eaten away'). The structure expresses a completed action by the stream on the stone.
segments.inferno-34-g149: col corso ch’elli avvolge,: col is the contraction of con + il ('with the'); corso = 'course' or 'flow' of the stream; ch’elli avvolge is a relative clause 'that it winds' (avvolge, present indicative, here 'winds' or 'twists'). The whole phrase explains how the stream has gnawed the stone — by the action of its meandering course.
segments.inferno-34-g150: e poco pende.: e 'and' connects to the previous clause; poco is an adverb meaning 'a little' or 'slightly'; pende is present indicative of pendere meaning 'hangs' or 'slopes'. Together it means the place 'slopes only slightly' (i.e. the angle is small).
segments.inferno-34-g151: Lo duca e io per quel cammino ascoso intrammo a ritornar nel chiaro mondo;: Lo duca e io is the compound subject ('the guide and I'); intrammo is passato remoto (first-person plural) 'we entered' or 'we went in'. Per quel cammino ascoso is a prepositional phrase modifying the manner/path; a ritornar is an infinitive of purpose ('to return'); nel chiaro mondo is the destination ('into the bright/clear world').
segments.inferno-34-g152: e sanza cura aver d’alcun riposo,: e 'and' connects clauses; sanza is an archaic preposition meaning 'without'; cura aver is an infinitival noun phrase 'having care/concern' (here 'without concern' or 'without care for'); d'alcun riposo is a partitive/indefinite 'of any rest' — together: 'and without any thought of rest'.
segments.inferno-34-g153: salimmo sù,: salimmo is passato remoto (first-person plural) 'we climbed/ascended'; sù is the particle 'up' (emphatic directional adverb). The short clipped phrase states the physical ascent.
segments.inferno-34-g154: el primo e io secondo,: elliptical phrase giving order of ascent. El (archaic il) primo (he) e io secondo (and I second) — predicates in nominal form indicating sequence; note the archaic article el instead of modern il and the elliptical omission of a verb ('went'/'followed' understood).
segments.inferno-34-g155: tanto ch’i’ vidi de le cose belle che porta ’l ciel,: tanto che introduces a result clause 'so that'; i' (elided form ch'i') is part of the contraction ch'i' = che io; vidi is passato remoto 'I saw'. De le cose belle = 'of the beautiful things' (partitive), and che porta 'l ciel is a relative clause modifying 'cose belle' with porta in the present indicative ('that heaven bears' or 'that the sky brings'). The whole expresses the result of the ascent: I saw some of heaven's beautiful things.
segments.inferno-34-g156: per un pertugio tondo.: per indicates motion through or by means of ('through a round aperture'); pertugio is a small opening or hole; tondo is an adjective 'round'. The prepositional phrase specifies how the things of heaven were visible (through a round opening).
segments.inferno-34-g157: E quindi uscimmo a riveder le stelle.: E 'and' connects; quindi here means 'from there' or 'then' indicating motion out of the prior place; uscimmo is passato remoto 'we went out' or 'we emerged'; a riveder is an infinitive of purpose/intent 'to see again' (riveder = rivedere), and le stelle is the object 'the stars.' The phrase is a succinct closing: they emerge to see the stars once more.
segments.inferno-34-g158: A single-word heading. 'Purgatorio' is the noun 'Purgatory' used as the title of the next section; capitalization in English indicates the proper-name sense.
segments.inferno-34-g82: 'Com'' is the apocopated form of 'come' meaning 'as'; it introduces an impersonal or concessive clause. 'a lui' is a dative ('to him') and 'piacque' is the passato remoto, 3rd person singular of 'piacere' — literally 'it pleased him', idiomatically 'as he wished'. Word order is typical poetic inversion.
segments.inferno-34-g83: Implied subject 'I' performs the action; 'il collo' is the direct object ('the neck'), 'li' is the dative pronoun 'to him' (referring back to the creature), and 'avvinghiai' is the passato remoto first-person singular of 'avvinghiare' meaning 'I clasped tightly / entwined around'. The verb after object is normal in Dante's style.
segments.inferno-34-g84: 'Ed el prese' = 'and he seized/took' with 'prese' passato remoto 3rd singular of 'prendere'. The phrase 'di tempo e loco poste' is elliptical/poetic: 'di' introduces 'tempo e loco' (time and place) and 'poste' functions like an adjective/participle ('placed' / 'stationed' / 'set'), indicating that the time and place were ready or appointed when he acted.
segments.inferno-34-g85: 'Quando' introduces a temporal clause. 'l'ali' ('the wings') is the subject; 'fuoro' is the passato remoto plural of 'essere' ('were'), and 'aperte' is the past participle agreeing with 'ali' — 'were opened'. 'assai' is an adverb intensifier here ('very' / 'wide/openly').
segments.inferno-34-g86: 'Appigliò' is the passato remoto 3rd singular of 'appigliare' (here reflexive in sense), 'sé' is the reflexive/emphatic pronoun 'himself'. The preposition 'a' + 'le vellute coste' marks what he grasped: 'le vellute' ('velvety', used adjectivally) modifies 'coste' ('ribs, sides'). The sequence shows a reflexive/centring motion: he fastened himself to the velvety ribs.
segments.inferno-34-g87: 'Di vello in vello' is a repetitive prepositional phrase meaning 'from fleece to fleece' (movement from one tuft to the next): here 'di' functions as 'from' and 'in' as the repeated linking preposition. 'Giù discese' uses 'discese' (passato remoto 3rd sing.) with 'giù' ('down') and 'poscia' ('afterwards/then'). The final prepositional phrase 'tra 'l folto pelo e le gelate croste' locates the descent 'between the thick hair and the frozen crusts'.
segments.inferno-34-g88: 'Quando noi fummo' uses 'fummo' (passato remoto, 1st person plural) 'we were'. 'Là' is 'there'; 'dove' introduces a relative/location clause 'where the thigh (la coscia) si volge' — 'si volge' is reflexive/present (or gnomic present) of 'volgere' meaning 'turns' or 'bends' (the place where the thigh turns).
segments.inferno-34-g89: 'a punto' = 'at the exact point' or 'just at'; 'in sul' is a double prepositional locution (poetic/archaic) meaning 'on' (in + su). 'il grosso de l'anche' = 'the bulk/roundness of the haunch' — here 'grosso' is a substantive/adjective 'bulk' qualifying 'anche' (haunches). Note: in this line 'anche' is a noun ('haunch'), not the common adverb 'anche'.
segments.inferno-34-g90: 'Lo duca' is the nominative label 'the leader/guide' (Virgil). The article 'lo' signals a masculine singular noun and stands before the title/noun as usual.
segments.inferno-34-g91: Simple prepositional phrase 'con fatica e con angoscia' = 'with fatigue and with anguish'; repetition of 'con' is stylistic and coordinates two instrumental circumstances describing the manner in which the guide acts.
segments.inferno-34-g92: 'Volse' is passato remoto 3rd singular of 'volgere' ('he turned'); 'la testa' is the direct object ('his head'). 'Ov'' (short for 'ove') = 'where'. 'Elli' in classical Tuscan would be a personal pronoun ('he' or 'him'), but the provided sense list does not include that meaning (see senses). 'Avea' is the imperfect of 'avere' ('he had') and 'le zanche' ('the shanks/legs') is the object of 'avea' — so 'he turned his head where he had his shanks/legs'.
segments.inferno-34-g93: 'E aggrappossi' = 'and he clung' (passato remoto with reflexive suffix; Dante's form 'aggrappossi' = 'si aggrappò'). 'Al pel' = 'to the hair/fur' ('pel' = 'pelo'). 'Com' om che sale' is a simile: 'as a man who climbs' — 'com'' = 'like/as', 'om' = 'man', 'che sale' relative clause 'who climbs/ascends'.
segments.inferno-34-g94: 'Sì che' introduces a result clause ('so that'). '’n inferno' = 'in Hell' (contraction of 'in'); 'i’' is the poet's contracted 'io' ('I'); 'credea' is the imperfect of 'creder' ('I thought'); 'tornar' is the infinitive 'to return'; 'anche' here means 'also/even' — the whole: 'so that I thought I was going back to Hell as well.'
segments.inferno-34-g95: 'Attienti' is an imperative form (archaic/poetic) from 'attener(si)/attienere', here meaning 'hold on' or 'cling fast'; 'ben' is the adverb 'well' or 'tight' — together an exhortation: 'hold on well/tightly'.
segments.inferno-34-g96: 'Ché' is a causal conjunction ('for/because'); 'per cotali scale' = 'by/through such stairs/steps' — 'cotali' is an archaic demonstrative/adjective 'such', and 'scale' is plural 'stairs/ladder'. The line explains why they must hold on.
segments.inferno-34-g97: 'Disse' is passato remoto 3rd singular of 'dire' ('he said'); '’l maestro' ('the master') is the speaker's title — the short article '’l' is the elided 'il'.
segments.inferno-34-g98: 'Ansando' is the present participle/gerund of 'ansare' used adverbially ('panting' or 'gasping'); 'com' = 'as', 'uom lasso' = 'a weary man' — descriptive phrase indicating how the master spoke: panting like a tired man.
segments.inferno-34-g99: 'Conviensi' (impersonal present form of 'convenire') means 'it is fitting/necessary'; 'dipartir' is the infinitive 'to depart/leave'; 'da tanto male' = 'from so much evil' — 'tanto' intensifies 'male' ('evil/harm'). The phrase is an impersonal prescription: 'it is necessary to depart from such great evil.'
segments.inferno-34-g100: 'Poi' = 'then/afterwards'. 'Uscì fuor' is a pleonastic combination ('went out outside') with 'uscì' passato remoto of 'uscire' and 'fuor' reinforcing 'out'. 'Per lo fóro d’un sasso' = 'through the hole of a rock' (note the article and poetic elision). 'E puose me in su l’orlo a sedere' — 'puose' is passato remoto of 'porre' ('he placed/put'); the object pronoun 'me' follows the verb here. 'In su l'orlo a sedere' = 'on the rim so that I might sit' (literal 'to sit').
segments.inferno-34-g101: 'Appresso' here means 'nearby' or 'then/after' (a sequence/adjacency adverb). 'Porse' is passato remoto of 'porgere' ('he offered/held out'); 'a me' marks the indirect object 'to me'. 'L'accorto passo' — 'l'' = article, 'accorto' = 'careful/shrewd/guarded' qualifying 'passo' = 'step' or 'path' — together 'the cautious step/path' (i.e., the careful place to step).

sentences.inferno-34-s5: When we had come so far on that it pleased my master to show me the creature that had the fair countenance, it was removed from before me and made me stand still, saying, “Behold Dis,” and “behold the place where you must arm yourself with fortitude.”
sentences.inferno-34-s6: How I then became frozen and faint — do not ask, reader, for I do not write it; because every speech would be too little.
sentences.inferno-34-s7: I neither died nor remained alive. Think now for your sake, if you have any flower of intellect, what I became, deprived of both one and the other.
sentences.inferno-34-s8: The Emperor of the dolorous kingdom came forth out of the ice up to the middle of his chest; and moreover I met a giant, with whose arms even giants do not compare: see now how great that whole must be which so fits to such a part.
sentences.inferno-34-s33: You were on that side as far as I had descended; when I turned, you had passed the point from which the weights are drawn on every side.
sentences.inferno-34-s34: And now you have come beneath the hemisphere that is opposite to that which the great dry land covers, and under whose worn summit was the man who was born and lived without sin; you have your feet upon a small sphere whose other face makes the Giudecca.
sentences.inferno-34-s35: Here it is on the right-hand side, when over there it is evening; and these, who made a ladder of it with their hair, are still as tightly set as they were before.
sentences.inferno-34-s36: From this side he fell down from heaven; and the earth, which had previously projected itself on this side, out of fear of him made a veil of the sea and came to our hemisphere; and perhaps, to flee him, left here an empty place that appears on this side, and ran back up.
sentences.inferno-34-s1: 'The banners of the King of Hell advance toward us; so look ahead,' my master said, 'if you can discern it.'
sentences.inferno-34-s2: As when a thick fog blows, or when our hemisphere grows dark, a mill that the wind turns seems from far off; I seemed to see such a building then; afterwards, because of the wind I drew back behind my leader, for there was no other grotto there.
sentences.inferno-34-s3: It already existed, and with fear I put it into verse, there where all the shades were covered, and they showed through like a straw in glass.
sentences.inferno-34-s4: Some lie prostrate; others stand upright, one with the head and another with the soles (of the feet); another, like a bow, turns his face toward his feet.
sentences.inferno-34-s9: If he was so beautiful as he is now ugly, and he raised his brows against his Maker, then every grief must indeed proceed from him.
sentences.inferno-34-s10: Oh, how great a wonder it seemed to me when I saw three faces on his head!
sentences.inferno-34-s11: One faced forward, and that one was vermilion; the other two were joined to it above the middle of each shoulder, and they met at the place of the crest: the right appeared between white and yellow; the left, in appearance, was like those that come from where the Nile slopes down.
sentences.inferno-34-s12: Beneath each of them two great wings protruded, as befits so great a bird: I never saw at sea sails like those.
sentences.inferno-34-s17: "That soul up there who suffers more," said the master, "is Judas Iscariot, who has his head inside and his legs outside."
sentences.inferno-34-s18: Of the other two who have their heads below, the one that hangs from the dark face is Brutus: see how he twists himself, and he makes no sound!
sentences.inferno-34-s19: ; and the other is Cassius, who appears so big‑limbed.
sentences.inferno-34-s20: But the night rises again, and now it is time to depart, for we have seen everything."
sentences.inferno-34-s13: They had no feathers, but their manner was like that of a bat; and those (wings) fluttered, so that three winds were set in motion from it: therefore all of Cocytus froze.
sentences.inferno-34-s14: He wept with six eyes, and from three mouths the tears and bloody froth dripped.
sentences.inferno-34-s15: From every mouth there burst forth, with its teeth, a sinner, like a crusher, so that he made three of them thus sorrowful.
sentences.inferno-34-s16: Compared with the one in front, the biting was nothing compared to the scratching, which at times left the back of the skin entirely bare.
sentences.inferno-34-s25: I lifted my eyes and thought I saw Lucifer as I had left him, and I saw his legs held up; and if I then became tormented, let the coarse crowd imagine it, who do not see which point I had passed.
sentences.inferno-34-s26: "Get up," said the master, "to your feet: the road is long and the path is evil, and already the sun returns at the half of the third hour."
sentences.inferno-34-s27: It was not a palace passage where we were, but a natural hollow that had bad ground and little light.
sentences.inferno-34-s28: 'Before he tears me from the abyss, my master,' I said when I stood upright, 'speak to pull me a little from error: where is the ice?'
sentences.inferno-34-s29: And these, how are they fixed so upside-down?
sentences.inferno-34-s30: And how, in so short a time, has the sun made the journey from evening to morning?
sentences.inferno-34-s32: And he to me: "Do you still imagine yourself to be on the other side of the center, where I seized by the hair the guilty worm that had bored the world."
sentences.inferno-34-s37: There is a place far down, remote from Beelzebub, as far as the tomb stretches, which is not known by sight but by the sound of a little stream that there descends through the hollow of a stone, which it has gnawed with the course it winds, and which slopes only a little.
sentences.inferno-34-s38: The guide and I entered by that hidden path to return to the bright world; and, without any thought of rest, we climbed up — he first and I second — so that I saw some of the fair things that heaven bears through a round aperture.
sentences.inferno-34-s39: And then we went out again to behold the stars.
sentences.inferno-34-s21: As he wished, I threw my arms about his neck; and he took advantage of the time and place set, and when the wings were opened wide he caught hold of the velvety ribs; from fleece to fleece he then descended down between the thick hair and the icy crusts.
sentences.inferno-34-s22: When we were there where the thigh turns, at the very point on the bulk of the haunch, the guide, with toil and anguish, turned his head where he had his shanks, and clung to the hair like a man who climbs, so that I thought even to return to Hell.
sentences.inferno-34-s23: "Hold on tight," said the master, panting like a weary man, "for by such ladders it is necessary to depart from so much evil."
sentences.inferno-34-s24: Then he went out through the opening of a rock and set me up on the edge to sit; nearby he offered me the careful step.
